message.content: Adversaries may exploit the User Account Control (UAC) mechanism in Windows to elevate process privileges without user consent. UAC is designed to prevent unauthorized changes by prompting for administrator approval. However, if UAC is not set to its highest level, certain programs can elevate privileges without prompting. Common methods include using Rundll32 to load a crafted DLL, injecting malicious software into trusted processes, or leveraging auto-elevated Component Object Model objects. Numerous techniques exist for bypassing UAC, with many documented on platforms like GitHub's UACME. Adversaries such as APT29, APT37, and various malware families (e.g., BitPaymer, BlackEnergy, Cobalt Strike) have been known to exploit these vulnerabilities. Mitigation strategies include setting UAC to its highest enforcement level, monitoring for unusual process behaviors, and employing application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized software execution. Regularly updating and patching systems can also help reduce the risk of UAC bypass exploits.
